title: Design Google Docs — Real-Time Collaboration
description: Design a real-time collaborative document editor. Covers operational transformation (OT) vs CRDTs for conflict resolution, WebSocket architecture for live cursors, multi-layer undo, offline editing with sync, branching/version history, and how SAP's enqueue locking and transport conflicts provide a conceptual bridge to understanding reconciliation in distributed editing.

description.value:
The fundamental challenge is: two users edit the same document at the same time. User A inserts 'X' at position 5. User B inserts 'Y' at position 5. Without coordination, the final document could be 'abXYZdef' or 'abYXZdef' or 'abZYXdef' depending on who processed the operations in which order. The system must ensure that all users converge to the same final document state.

Two approaches exist — OT vs CRDT:

Operational Transformation (OT) — used by Google Docs: Each user's edits are represented as operations (insert 'X' at position 5). When two users' operations conflict, the server transforms one operation to account for the other. Example: User A inserts 'X' at position 5. User B inserts 'Y' at position 3. The server receives both operations. If A's operation is applied first, then B's 'insert Y at position 3' remains position 3 (the 'X' was inserted at position 5, which doesn't affect position 3). If B's operation is applied first, then A's 'insert X at position 5' must be transformed to 'insert X at position 6' (because B inserted 'Y' at position 3, shifting all subsequent positions by 1). OT is complex (every pair of operation types requires a transformation function) but proven in production since Google Wave (2009) and Google Docs (2012).

CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) — used by Google Docs (recently), Figma, VS Code Live Share: Data is stored in a structure where concurrent operations commute (the final state is independent of operation order). CRDTs avoid the need for a central transformation server. Each client has a copy of the document; clients merge changes locally using CRDT merge rules. Google Docs recently migrated from OT to CRDT for offline support. CRDTs are simpler to implement correctly (no complex transformation functions) but use more memory (each character carries metadata — site ID, position in sequence).

SAP Bridge: SAP's lock mechanism (enqueue) prevents concurrent editing of the same record. Two users cannot edit the same sales order simultaneously because SAP LUW holds a lock on the document. Google Docs takes the opposite approach: allow concurrent editing and reconcile conflicts. The SAP engineer familiar with 'transport conflicts' (two developers transporting the same object) has experienced a similar problem — two people changed the same ABAP object, and the transport merge failed. Google Docs automates this merge process for text.

The synchronization layer propagates document changes between users in real-time (sub-200ms). It must handle: concurrent edits, network disconnections, reconnections with pending changes, and multiple device support (phone, tablet, desktop).

Architecture components:

1. WebSocket connection: Each client establishes a persistent WebSocket connection to the collaboration server. The connection carries: document changes (operations), cursor positions (broadcasted to other users), and presence information (who is viewing). Google uses a custom WebSocket-like protocol over HTTP/2 for reliability.

2. Collaboration server (session host): Each document has a 'session host' — a server process responsible for that document's OT/CRDT merge logic. The session host: (a) Receives operations from all connected clients. (b) Orders operations (using a central sequence number — 'revision ID'). (c) Applies OT or CRDT merge to produce the canonical document state. (d) Broadcasts the canonical operation to all clients. Clients update their local state to match the canonical state. The session host is stateful — it holds the current document state in memory for fast access.

3. Document store: Persistent storage for the document content. The canonical document state is periodically persisted to a database (Google's Spanner for global consistency). On session host crash, a new session host loads the latest persisted state and continues. The persistence interval is a tradeoff: too frequent = high write load on Spanner; too infrequent = more data loss on crash. Google persists every few seconds or after every N operations.

4. Offline support: When a user loses connectivity, their local CRDT stores pending operations in a local IndexedDB. When connectivity returns, the pending operations are sent to the server. The server merges them (CRDT commute) and returns the merged state. If OT is used instead of CRDT, offline support is more complex (pending operations must be transformed against operations from other users that arrived during the disconnection). This is the primary reason Google Docs is migrating to CRDT.

Live cursors show where other users are editing in real-time. This is a pure UX feature but involves interesting technical constraints.

Cursor broadcast: Each client sends its cursor position (character offset) to the session host every ~50ms (debounced). The session host broadcasts cursor positions to all other connected clients. The broadcast rate must be limited: 50ms = 20 broadcasts/second/user. For 100 concurrent editors on a document, that's 2000 broadcasts/second. This is manageable but must be handled efficiently (binary WebSocket messages, not JSON).

Cursor position after edits: Cursor positions must be transformed along with document edits. If User A is at position 50 and User B inserts 10 characters at position 20, User A's cursor position must be moved from 50 to 60. This is part of the OT/CRDT transformation. In CRDT, cursor positions are represented as references to the underlying characters (using the character's unique CRDT identifier), not numeric offsets. This makes cursor adjustment implicit (the cursor follows the character it's attached to).

SAP Bridge: SAP's 'lock indicator' on screen fields serves a similar purpose — it shows which fields another user is editing. But SAP's implementation is pessimistic (lock prevents concurrent editing), not optimistic (allow concurrent editing and reconcile). The concept of 'presence awareness' is the same: the system must communicate who is doing what, in real-time.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: The Google Docs question tests: (1) Your understanding of distributed state reconciliation (OT vs CRDT). (2) Your awareness of real-time protocol design (WebSocket, session hosts, broadcast). (3) Your ability to design for offline-first (local CRDT, sync on reconnect). Staff+ interviewers at Google love this question because it probes edge-case thinking: 'What happens when three users insert at the same position simultaneously?' or 'How do you recover from a session host crash with in-flight operations?' The SAP engineer with transport conflict experience has a compelling bridge: 'I've dealt with merge conflicts in ABAP transports. Google Docs solves the same problem, at a different level of abstraction.'
